name: Admiral Grigorovich-class frigate
overview_md:
The Admiral Grigorovich-class frigates (Project 11356R/M) represent Russia's attempt to field a modern, blue-water capable surface combatant in the wake of the Soviet Union's collapse. Based on the proven Krivak-III hull design but incorporating modern sensors and weapons systems, these frigates were designed to provide the Russian Navy with credible anti-surface, anti-air, and anti-submarine capabilities at a reasonable cost.

Strategically, the class was intended to restore Russia's ability to project naval power beyond its immediate coastal waters, particularly in the Black Sea and Mediterranean. The design philosophy emphasized multi-role capability while maintaining production feasibility given Russia's constrained post-Soviet shipbuilding industry. However, the program has been severely hampered by sanctions following Russia's 2014 annexation of Crimea, which cut off access to Ukrainian-built gas turbine engines.

In the current threat environment, these frigates matter primarily as symbols of Russian naval ambition rather than game-changing capabilities. While modern by Russian standards, they lag significantly behind Western contemporaries in areas like radar performance, electronic warfare systems, and overall systems integration. The class has seen extensive combat deployment in Syria, providing both naval gunfire support and cruise missile strikes.

Compared to peer frigates like the Type 054A or FREMM classes, the Grigorovich suffers from limited VLS capacity, older radar technology, and questionable build quality. The program's truncation due to engine supply issues—only 3 of 6 planned hulls were completed for the Russian Navy—underscores the broader challenges facing Russian naval modernization efforts.

specs_json.propulsion: CODAG: 2x M7N.1E gas turbines, 2x M-507A diesels

armament_json.name: Package-NK
armament_json.notes: Can fire torpedoes or ASW rockets

variants_json.name: Project 11356R
variants_json.designation_range: Admiral Grigorovich, Admiral Essen, Admiral Makarov
variants_json.key_changes: Initial production version with Ukrainian M7N.1E gas turbines, Fregat-M2EM radar, 24-cell Shtil-1 VLS

variants_json.name: Project 11356M
variants_json.designation_range: Admiral Butakov, Admiral Istomin, Admiral Kornilov
variants_json.key_changes: Planned Russian-built engines to replace Ukrainian systems, construction halted and hulls sold to India

news.title: Damen launches ‘Drone Carrier’ for the Portuguese Navy
news.snippet: Damen Shipyards Group has launched the NRP D. João II a Multi-Purpose Vessel (MPV) 10720 for the Portuguese Navy. The vessel entered the water at Damen Shipyards Galati in Romania on April 7. Damen press release The launch event took place in the presence of Vice Admiral Pires on behalf of the Portu

combat_history.event: Admiral Grigorovich conducted first combat deployment to Syria, providing naval gunfire support with A-190 gun system against ISIS positions near Palmyra
combat_history.significance: Marked the class's combat debut and demonstrated Russia's renewed expeditionary naval capabilities

combat_history.event: Admiral Essen launched Kalibr cruise missiles at ISIS targets in Syria, marking the class's first use of its primary long-range strike weapon
combat_history.significance: Validated the Kalibr system's effectiveness and showcased Russia's precision strike capability from naval platforms

combat_history.event: Admiral Grigorovich and Admiral Essen participated in naval task force responding to US/UK/French strikes on Syrian chemical weapons facilities
combat_history.significance: Demonstrated Russia's ability to maintain persistent naval presence in contested waters during crisis

combat_history.event: Class vessels reportedly participated in blockade operations in Black Sea, though specific engagements remain unclear due to operational security
combat_history.significance: First major conventional naval conflict deployment, testing systems against modern Western-supplied weapons

modernization.description: Attempted to develop Russian-built gas turbines to replace Ukrainian M7N.1E engines after 2014 sanctions. Program failed due to technical difficulties.
modernization.impact: Limited production to 3 hulls, forced sale of remaining hulls to India

modernization.description: Reported installation of improved EW systems and decoy launchers on existing hulls during maintenance periods
modernization.impact: Marginal improvement in survivability against Western anti-ship missiles

modernization.description: Potential upgrade to fire Tsirkon hypersonic missiles from existing 3S-14 VLS cells
modernization.impact: Would significantly enhance strike capability if technically feasible

vulnerabilities.description: Limited to 24 medium-range SAMs with older Shtil-1 system lacking capability against modern supersonic anti-ship missiles
vulnerabilities.context: Vulnerable to saturation attacks from Western or Chinese anti-ship missiles like LRASM or YJ-18
vulnerabilities.mitigation: Relies on layered defense with AK-630M CIWS and electronic warfare, but gaps remain significant

vulnerabilities.description: EW systems appear limited compared to Western standards, with poor integration with combat management system
vulnerabilities.context: Critical weakness against NATO forces with advanced electronic attack capabilities
vulnerabilities.mitigation: Ongoing upgrades reported but specifics classified and effectiveness uncertain

vulnerabilities.description: Cannot produce additional hulls due to Ukrainian engine embargo, limiting fleet size and spare parts availability
vulnerabilities.context: Represents broader Russian defense industrial weakness in complex systems integration
vulnerabilities.mitigation: Engine replacement program failed; no current solution for series production

vulnerabilities.description: Sonar suite and processing systems lag significantly behind Western equivalents, limited helicopter ASW capability
vulnerabilities.context: Major vulnerability against modern conventional submarines in Mediterranean or North Atlantic operations
vulnerabilities.mitigation: Relies heavily on coordinated ASW operations with other platforms

operational_patterns.typical_deployment: Mediterranean rotation for Syrian support, Black Sea patrol and power projection
operational_patterns.typical_task_group: Single ship deployments or paired with support vessels and submarines
operational_patterns.readiness_notes: Maintenance challenging due to sanctions on Western components, limited dry dock availability

peer_comparisons.key_differences: Type 054A has superior radar, more VLS cells (32), and better production sustainability. Grigorovich has longer range and more powerful engines.
peer_comparisons.video_angle: East vs West: How sanctions shaped modern frigate development

peer_comparisons.key_differences: FREMM significantly superior in sensors, combat systems, and stealth. Grigorovich cheaper but far less capable in modern warfare scenarios.
peer_comparisons.video_angle: NATO vs Russian frigate philosophy: quality vs quantity

peer_comparisons.key_differences: Type 26 represents generational leap with integrated electric propulsion, advanced sonar, 48 VLS cells. Grigorovich already obsolete by comparison.
peer_comparisons.video_angle: Future frigate warfare: why Russia is falling behind

peer_comparisons.key_differences: Essentially identical hull and systems, but Indian variants may have different electronics fit and maintenance standards.
peer_comparisons.video_angle: Export success: How Russian frigates serve different navies

doctrine.role: Regional sea control and power projection within Russia's near abroad, designed to restore blue-water capability lost after the Soviet collapse while operating under land-based air cover.
doctrine.employment: Typically deployed as flagship of surface action groups in the Black Sea and Mediterranean, conducting freedom of navigation operations and supporting Russian interests in Syria and Africa. Often operates independently or with one auxiliary due to fleet size constraints, relying on shore-based air support rather than organic air defence. Command structure emphasizes political visibility missions alongside traditional naval warfare roles.
doctrine.design_philosophy: Prioritized proven hull design and cost control over cutting-edge capability, accepting reduced magazine depth and limited growth potential to field hulls quickly. Designers sacrificed comprehensive multi-warfare capability for focused anti-surface warfare, relying on Kalibr missiles as the primary offensive system while accepting minimal close-in weapons systems.
doctrine.threat_context: Designed for Mediterranean and Black Sea operations against NATO surface groups with assumed air parity, but now faces Western navies with comprehensive missile defence and superior electronic warfare capabilities. The threat environment has shifted toward distributed lethality and long-range precision strike, challenging the platform's relatively limited defensive systems.
doctrine.comparison_frame: Compare on missile range and political utility rather than sustained combat capability - these are strategic messaging platforms first, warships second. Magazine depth and defensive systems matter less than Kalibr strike range and ability to show the flag in contested waters.
